# Methods

## Overview

This systematic review was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) 2020 guidelines. The review synthesized the existing literature on hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) in adult populations, encompassing pathogenesis, diagnostic approaches, etiological classification, treatment strategies, and emerging associations with novel triggers. The protocol was developed a priori, specifying the research questions, search strategy, eligibility criteria, quality assessment methods, and data synthesis framework.

## Search Strategy

A comprehensive literature search was performed across three major biomedical databases: Scopus, PubMed, and Embase, selected to ensure broad coverage of clinical, immunological, and translational research pertinent to adult HLH. The search was restricted to publications from January 2016 through March 2026.

The search strategy employed a combination of Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) terms and free-text keywords. The primary search string was: ("hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis" OR "hemophagocytic syndrome" OR "macrophage activation syndrome") AND ("adult" OR "adults"). HLH and macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) were combined as synonymous terms given their overlapping pathophysiology. The search was applied to titles, abstracts, and keyword fields across all three databases, with no restrictions on study design at the initial stage. Reference lists of included articles were also manually screened to identify additional eligible studies.

## Study Selection and PRISMA Flow

The study selection process followed a systematic, multi-stage screening protocol. All records retrieved from the three databases were imported into reference management software, where duplicate entries were identified and removed. The initial search yielded 897 records, of which 114 were duplicates, leaving 783 unique records for screening. Two reviewers independently screened titles and abstracts for relevance, excluding 612 records that did not meet the broad inclusion criteria. The remaining 171 full-text articles were assessed for eligibility against the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Of these, 121 were excluded for the following reasons: CiteScore below 3.0 (n = 43), case reports involving fewer than five patients (n = 31), editorials or commentaries (n = 18), pediatric-only populations (n = 16), non-English language (n = 8), and insufficient relevance to the review objectives (n = 5). This process yielded 50 articles published across 36 journals for inclusion in the qualitative synthesis. Disagreements between reviewers at any stage were resolved through discussion and consensus.

The following PRISMA flow diagram summarizes the selection process.

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node distance=12mm and 15mm,
  mynode/.style={draw, rectangle, align=center, minimum height=10ex, text width=5.5cm, font=\small, outer sep=0pt},
  exnode/.style={draw, rectangle, align=left, minimum height=10ex, text width=7cm, font=\small, outer sep=0pt},
  ml/.style args={#1/#2}{draw, rectangle, align=center, rounded corners, font=\small\bfseries, inner sep=2ex, outer sep=0pt, fill=cyan!30, minimum height=1.8*10ex, left=8mm of #2},
  arrow/.style={thick,->,>=stealth}
]
\coordinate (tc);

% Identification Phase
\node[mynode, left=of tc] (n1a) {Records identified through\\database searching\\(Scopus + PubMed + Embase)\\(\textbf{n = 897})};
\node[mynode, right=of tc] (n1b) {Additional records identified\\through other sources\\(\textbf{n = 0})};
\node[mynode, below=15mm of tc |- n1a.south] (n2) {Records after duplicates removed\\(\textbf{n = 783})};
\draw[arrow] (n1a) -- (n2);
\draw[arrow] (n1b) -- (n2);

% Screening Phase
\node[mynode, below=15mm of n2] (n3) {Records screened\\(title and abstract)\\(\textbf{n = 783})};
\node[exnode, right=20mm of n3] (n3r) {Records excluded\\(\textbf{n = 612})};
\draw[arrow] (n2) -- (n3);
\draw[arrow] (n3) -- (n3r);

% Eligibility Phase
\node[mynode, below=15mm of n3] (n4) {Full-text articles assessed\\for eligibility\\(\textbf{n = 171})};
\node[exnode, right=20mm of n4, text width=7.5cm] (n4r) {Full-text articles excluded (\textbf{n = 121})\\[3pt]
\quad $\bullet$ CiteScore $<$ 3.0 (n = 43)\\
\quad $\bullet$ Case reports $<$ 5 patients (n = 31)\\
\quad $\bullet$ Editorials/commentaries (n = 18)\\
\quad $\bullet$ Pediatric-only (n = 16)\\
\quad $\bullet$ Non-English (n = 8)\\
\quad $\bullet$ Insufficient relevance (n = 5)};
\draw[arrow] (n3) -- (n4);
\draw[arrow] (n4) -- (n4r);

% Inclusion Phase
\node[mynode, below=15mm of n4] (n5) {Studies included in\\qualitative synthesis\\(\textbf{n = 50})\\(across 36 journals)};
\draw[arrow] (n4) -- (n5);

% Phase Labels
\node[ml=1.8*10ex/n1a.west] {\rotatebox{90}{\textbf{Identification}}};
\node[ml=1.8*10ex/{n1a.west |- {$(n2)!0.5!(n3)$}}] {\rotatebox{90}{\textbf{Screening}}};
\node[ml=1.8*10ex/{n1a.west |- {$(n3)!0.5!(n4)$}}] {\rotatebox{90}{\textbf{Eligibility}}};
\node[ml=1.8*10ex/{n1a.west |- n5}] {\rotatebox{90}{\textbf{Included}}};

\end{tikzpicture}
}
\caption{PRISMA 2020 flow diagram for the systematic review of hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis in adults.}
\label{fig-prisma}
\end{figure}
```

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
![PRISMA Flow Diagram](prisma-flow.png){#fig-prisma}
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**PRISMA Flow:** 897 records identified → 783 after deduplication → 171 full-text assessed → 50 included in synthesis (36 journals). Excluded: CiteScore < 3.0 (n=43), case reports < 5 patients (n=31), editorials (n=18), pediatric-only (n=16), non-English (n=8), insufficient relevance (n=5).
:::

## Inclusion Criteria

Articles were included in the final review if they met all of the following criteria. First, the publication appeared in a peer-reviewed journal with a CiteScore of 3.0 or higher, ensuring a baseline threshold of journal quality and scholarly impact. Second, the article was published in the English language. Third, the study addressed hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis, hemophagocytic syndrome, or macrophage activation syndrome with direct relevance to adult populations, including studies that encompassed both pediatric and adult cohorts provided that adult-specific data or conclusions could be extracted. Fourth, the study presented original research, systematic reviews, meta-analyses, large retrospective analyses, or comprehensive narrative reviews contributing substantive evidence to the understanding of adult HLH. Studies of any geographic origin were eligible, and no restrictions were placed on study setting (inpatient, outpatient, or registry-based).

## Exclusion Criteria

Articles were excluded if they met any of the following conditions: case reports or case series involving fewer than five patients, given the limited generalizability of such small samples; editorials, letters to the editor, commentaries, or opinion pieces lacking primary data or systematic methodology; studies focused exclusively on pediatric HLH populations without findings applicable or transferable to adult disease; publications in languages other than English for which verified translations were unavailable; and conference abstracts not subsequently published as full-length peer-reviewed articles. Studies addressing cytokine release syndrome or macrophage activation exclusively in the context of non-HLH conditions without drawing explicit connections to HLH pathophysiology were also excluded.

## Quality Assessment

A multi-dimensional quality assessment framework was applied to all 50 articles meeting the inclusion criteria. Journal-level quality was evaluated using CiteScore metrics, with the included articles spanning journals whose CiteScore values ranged from the 3.0 minimum threshold to high-impact specialty and general medical journals. Citation frequency served as an additional indicator of influence and uptake within the research community; the 50 included articles demonstrated citation counts ranging from 112 to 7,564, with a mean citation frequency of approximately 946, reflecting a corpus of well-established and frequently referenced works.

Study-level quality was assessed using validated instruments appropriate to each study type. Systematic reviews and meta-analyses were evaluated using the AMSTAR-2 checklist, appraising 16 domains including protocol registration, search comprehensiveness, and risk of bias assessment. Observational studies were appraised using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS), evaluating selection, comparability, and outcome ascertainment across a maximum of nine stars. Diagnostic accuracy studies were evaluated using the QUADAS-2 framework, assessing bias and applicability across four domains: patient selection, index test, reference standard, and flow and timing. Clinical practice guidelines were assessed based on development transparency, expert involvement, and evidence grading.

Each article was independently assessed by two reviewers, with discrepancies resolved by consensus. The overall certainty of the body of evidence for each thematic domain was assessed qualitatively using principles analogous to the GRADE (Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development and Evaluations) framework, considering the consistency of findings across studies, the directness of evidence, the precision of effect estimates where available, and the methodological rigor of contributing studies.

## Data Extraction and Synthesis

A standardized data extraction form was developed a priori to capture key variables from each included article, including study design, sample size, patient demographics, diagnostic criteria employed, etiological classifications, treatment protocols, clinical outcomes, and biomarker data. For studies addressing pathogenesis, specific data on cytokine pathways, genetic predispositions, and immunological mechanisms were recorded. Two reviewers independently extracted data, with discrepancies resolved through discussion. Data preparation involved standardizing extracted variables across heterogeneous study designs and harmonizing outcome definitions to enable cross-study comparison within each thematic domain.

Given the heterogeneity of study designs, patient populations, and outcome measures, a quantitative meta-analysis was not feasible. Instead, a thematic narrative synthesis was adopted, organizing findings into five principal domains: (1) pathogenesis and immunological mechanisms, (2) diagnostic criteria and biomarkers, (3) etiological classification and triggers, (4) treatment strategies and outcomes, and (5) HLH in the context of COVID-19 and emerging infections. Within each domain, evidence was synthesized by identifying convergent findings, areas of consensus, evolving paradigms, and remaining knowledge gaps.
